On the Topology of Continuous Functions

2026-04-01

We begin with a review of the fundamental structures underlying continuous mappings between topological spaces.

Preliminaries

Topological Spaces

Let \(X\) be a topological space equipped with a topology \(\tau\). Recall that \(\tau\) satisfies the usual axioms of closure under arbitrary unions and finite intersections.

Definition 1.1. A function \(f: X \to Y\) is continuous if for every open set \(V \subseteq Y\), the preimage \(f^{-1}(V)\) is open in \(X\).

\[f^{-1}(V) = \{x \in X \mid f(x) \in V\}\]

Compactness

Compactness is among the most powerful properties a topological space can possess.

Theorem 1.2. A continuous image of a compact space is compact.

The Intermediate Value Theorem

We now state and prove one of the cornerstones of analysis.

Theorem 2.1 (Intermediate Value Theorem). Let \(f: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}\) be continuous. If \(f(a) < d < f(b)\), then there exists \(c \in (a,b)\) such that \(f(c) = d\).

\[\forall\, d \in (f(a), f(b)),\; \exists\, c \in (a,b) : f(c) = d\]

Proof. Consider the set \(S = \{x \in [a,b] \mid f(x) < d\}\). Since \(f(a) < d\), the set \(S\) is nonempty. Let \(c = \sup S\). By continuity of \(f\), we conclude \(f(c) = d\). \(\blacksquare\)

A Geometric Illustration

The following diagram illustrates a continuous map between two spaces.
